phrasalVerbs.definition: To accept a job, responsibility, or challenge.
examples: She decided to take on the new project despite her busy schedule.
The company is taking on ten new employees this month.
Don't take on too much work — you'll get stressed.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To do or complete a task or plan.
examples: The team carried out a detailed review of the project.
Engineers will carry out the repairs next week.
It's important to carry out your duties carefully.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To establish or start something, such as a business or system.
examples: He set up his own company at the age of 25.
Can you set up a meeting for Thursday afternoon?
They set up a new office in the city centre.
The new manager brought about many positive changes.
Technology has brought about a revolution in communication.
What can we do to bring about better working conditions?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To handle or manage a problem, situation, or person.
examples: I need to deal with this complaint before the end of the day.
She's very good at dealing with difficult clients.
How do you deal with stress at work?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To use all of something so that there is none left; also, to have no more time.
examples: We've run out of printer paper — can you order more?
Hurry up! We're running out of time.
The project ran out of funding before it was finished.

fillInExercises.sentence: Our manager decided to ___ a new marketing strategy this quarter.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Carry out' means to complete or perform a task or plan.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ up her own bakery after years of working for someone else.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Set up' means to establish or start something.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new CEO ___ many changes that improved employee satisfaction.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Bring about' means to cause something to happen.

fillInExercises.sentence: He's great at ___ with difficult situations calmly and professionally.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Deal with' means to handle a situation or problem.

fillInExercises.sentence: We've ___ of coffee in the office kitchen. Can someone buy more?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Run out (of)' means to have no more of something left.

fillInExercises.sentence: The company decided to ___ on 20 new graduates this summer.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take on' means to hire or accept someone or something.

fillInExercises.sentence: How do you usually ___ stress when you have a busy deadline?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Deal with' means to manage or handle something.

fillInExercises.sentence: We need to ___ out all the safety checks before the factory opens.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Carry out' means to perform or complete a task.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Have you heard? The company wants to [take on] fifteen new developers.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: Really? I heard they also want to [set up] a new office in Madrid.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Yes! The director wants to [bring about] some big changes this year.

lines.speaker: Emma
lines.text: That's a lot to [deal with]. I hope they don't [run out] of budget.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Meanwhile, we still need to [carry out] the quarterly review.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: My interview is tomorrow and I might [run out] of things to say.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Don't worry! Talk about how you [deal with] challenges at work.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I can mention the project where I helped [set up] the new system.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: And explain how you [take on] extra responsibilities without being asked.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I also [bring about] some cost savings — I should mention that.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Exactly! Just [carry out] the interview as a normal conversation.
